title: Arrival/Departure Forms: I-94 and I-94W
source: U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP)
topics: arrival records, departure records, electronic I-94, travel, CBP, ESTA, Visa Waiver Program, employment authorization, immigration status
text:
Foreign visitors to the United States generally no longer need to complete paper Form I-94 (Arrival/Departure Record) or Form I-94W (Nonimmigrant Visa Waiver Arrival/Departure Record). U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) now automatically collects arrival and departure information from travelers' electronic travel records.

Travelers who need to prove their lawful visitor status to employers, educational institutions, or government agencies can access their arrival and departure records online through the CBP I-94 website or the CBP Link mobile application.

Electronic I-94 at Land Borders
- Travelers entering the United States by land are generally issued electronic I-94 records instead of paper forms.
- CBP encourages travelers who require an I-94 to apply in advance using:
  - The CBP I-94 website.
  - The CBP Link mobile application.
- Travelers using the Visa Waiver Program (VWP) must obtain an approved Electronic System for Travel Authorization (ESTA) before arriving at a U.

title: Arrival/Departure Forms: I-94 and I-94W
source: U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP)
topics: arrival records, departure records, electronic I-94, travel, CBP, ESTA, Visa Waiver Program, employment authorization, immigration status
text:
ion.
- Travelers using the Visa Waiver Program (VWP) must obtain an approved Electronic System for Travel Authorization (ESTA) before arriving at a U.S. land border.
- VWP travelers are encouraged to obtain their I-94 through the website or mobile app before travel.

Obtaining a Paper Copy
- Travelers who need a paper copy of their I-94 may print it themselves using the "Get Most Recent I-94" feature on the I-94 website or through the CBP Link mobile app.
- Travelers may also request a paper Form I-94 during the inspection process. Such requests are generally handled in a secondary inspection setting.

Using the I-94 for Status Verification
- Travelers who need to verify their immigration status or employment authorization should obtain their I-94 number from the CBP I-94 website or the CBP Link mobile application.

Departure Procedures
Paper I-94 Holders
- Travelers issued a paper Form I-94 should surrender it when departing the United States to:
  - The commercial carrier,
  - The Ca

title: Arrival/Departure Forms: I-94 and I-94W
source: U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP)
topics: arrival records, departure records, electronic I-94, travel, CBP, ESTA, Visa Waiver Program, employment authorization, immigration status
text:
aper I-94 Holders
- Travelers issued a paper Form I-94 should surrender it when departing the United States to:
  - The commercial carrier,
  - The Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA), or
  - U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP).

Electronic I-94 Holders
- Travelers departing by air or sea generally have their departure recorded automatically through carrier manifest information or CBP systems.
- Travelers departing by land may not always have their departure recorded automatically.

Land Departure Recommendations
If a traveler:
- Received an electronic I-94,
- Departs the United States by land,
- Is not a resident of Canada or Mexico, and
- Does not re-enter the United States before the admission expiration date,

CBP recommends keeping evidence of departure, which may include:
- Passport entry stamps.
- Transportation tickets.
- Pay stubs.
- Receipts.
- Other documentation showing departure from the United States.

Travelers may request:
- An entry stamp from the Canada Border S

title: Arrival/Departure Forms: I-94 and I-94W
source: U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP)
topics: arrival records, departure records, electronic I-94, travel, CBP, ESTA, Visa Waiver Program, employment authorization, immigration status
text:
y stubs.
- Receipts.
- Other documentation showing departure from the United States.

Travelers may request:
- An entry stamp from the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) when entering Canada.
- An entry stamp from Mexico's Instituto Nacional de Migración (INM) when entering Mexico.

Benefits of Electronic Processing
The electronic I-94 system:
- Streamlines the admission process.
- Improves security.
- Reduces administrative costs.
- Saves the federal government an estimated $15.5 million annually.

I-94 Number Format
Beginning in May 2019:
- I-94 numbers became alphanumeric.
- Each I-94 number contains 11 characters.
- Format:
  - Characters 1–9: digits.
  - Character 10: letter.
  - Character 11: digit.
- Previously issued numeric-only I-94 records remain valid until their listed Admit Until Date or the expiration shown on the official I-94 website.

title: I-94 Automation Fact Sheet
source: U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP)
topics: I-94 automation, arrival/departure record, electronic I-94, CBP One, travel history, immigration status, employment authorization
text:
Overview

To increase efficiency, reduce operating costs, and streamline the admissions process, U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) has automated Form I-94 at air, sea, and land ports of entry. Paper Forms I-94 are generally no longer issued upon arrival except in limited circumstances.

Travelers who require proof of their admission record, immigration status, alien registration, or employment authorization can retrieve and print their electronic I-94 from the CBP I-94 website or the CBP One mobile application.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is Form I-94?

Form I-94 is the U.S. Department of Homeland Security (DHS) arrival/departure record. It is issued electronically to travelers who:
- Are admitted to the United States.
- Adjust status while in the United States.
- Extend their immigration status.
- Require an official record of admission.

Travelers must depart the United States on or before the departure date shown on their I-94.

How does I-94 automation affect travelers

title: I-94 Automation Fact Sheet
source: U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP)
topics: I-94 automation, arrival/departure record, electronic I-94, CBP One, travel history, immigration status, employment authorization
text:
of admission.

Travelers must depart the United States on or before the departure date shown on their I-94.

How does I-94 automation affect travelers?

The automation of Form I-94 does not change a traveler's eligibility to enter the United States.

CBP continues to create an I-94 record for travelers who require one, but it is generated electronically instead of on paper.

If a paper copy is needed, travelers may print it from:
- The CBP I-94 website.
- The CBP One mobile application.

Will travelers receive proof of admission?

Yes.

Travelers receive an electronic I-94 record that can be retrieved online. CBP may also place an admission stamp in the travel document showing:
- Date of admission.
- Class of admission.
- Admit-until date.

How are departures recorded?

Travelers generally do not need to take additional steps when leaving the United States.

Air or Sea Departures
- CBP records departures electronically using carrier manifest information.

Land Departures
- A departure

title: I-94 Automation Fact Sheet
source: U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP)
topics: I-94 automation, arrival/departure record, electronic I-94, CBP One, travel history, immigration status, employment authorization
text:
the United States.

Air or Sea Departures
- CBP records departures electronically using carrier manifest information.

Land Departures
- A departure is recorded if the traveler departs by land and re-enters the United States before the I-94 expires.
- Travelers who are not residents of Canada or Mexico and who leave by land without re-entering before expiration should keep evidence of departure.

Examples of acceptable evidence include:
- Passport entry stamps.
- Transportation tickets.
- Pay stubs.
- Receipts.
- Other documentation proving departure.

Travelers entering Canada may request an entry stamp from the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA). Travelers entering Mexico may request an entry stamp from the Instituto Nacional de Migración (INM).

Travelers issued a paper Form I-94 should surrender it to:
- Their commercial carrier.
- CBSA when entering Canada by land.
- A CBP officer or designated drop box at the port of departure.

Travelers who still possess a paper I-94 after d

title: I-94 Automation Fact Sheet
source: U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP)
topics: I-94 automation, arrival/departure record, electronic I-94, CBP One, travel history, immigration status, employment authorization
text:
CBSA when entering Canada by land.
- A CBP officer or designated drop box at the port of departure.

Travelers who still possess a paper I-94 after departure should follow CBP instructions for returning it to ensure their departure is properly recorded.

Will CBP still issue paper Forms I-94?

Generally, no.

CBP primarily issues electronic I-94 records.

Paper Forms I-94 may still be issued for certain categories of travelers, including:
- Some asylees.
- Some parolees.
- Other situations where CBP determines a paper form is appropriate.

Refugee I-94 records issued after September 7, 2015, are processed electronically and can be retrieved online.

If a traveler cannot locate their electronic I-94, they should:
- Contact CBP through the CBP Help Center.
- Visit a local CBP Deferred Inspection Site or Port of Entry.

What is the CBP I-94 website?

The CBP I-94 website allows travelers to:
- Retrieve and print their electronic I-94.
- Obtain proof of lawful visitor status.
- View a lim

title: I-94 Automation Fact Sheet
source: U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP)
topics: I-94 automation, arrival/departure record, electronic I-94, CBP One, travel history, immigration status, employment authorization
text:
website?

The CBP I-94 website allows travelers to:
- Retrieve and print their electronic I-94.
- Obtain proof of lawful visitor status.
- View a limited history of U.S. arrivals and departures.
- Check travel compliance.

What is the CBP One mobile application?

CBP One is an official mobile application that provides access to multiple CBP services, including:
- Retrieving electronic I-94 records.
- Accessing I-94-related services available through CBP.

What if a traveler was admitted incorrectly?

Travelers who believe their admission information is incorrect should first verify their I-94 record through:
- The CBP I-94 website.
- The CBP One mobile application.

If the information remains incorrect, they should:
- Visit a CBP Deferred Inspection Site or Port of Entry to request a correction.

If an incorrect Form I-94 was issued by U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS), the traveler should refer to Form I-102 or contact the nearest USCIS office.

Key Benefits of I-94 A

title: I-94 Automation Fact Sheet
source: U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP)
topics: I-94 automation, arrival/departure record, electronic I-94, CBP One, travel history, immigration status, employment authorization
text:
.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS), the traveler should refer to Form I-102 or contact the nearest USCIS office.

Key Benefits of I-94 Automation
- Streamlines the admissions process.
- Reduces paper processing.
- Lowers operating costs.
- Provides online access to admission records.
- Supports verification of immigration status and employment authorization.
- Allows travelers to retrieve travel history electronically.

title: Students and the Form I-20
source: Study in the States (U.S. Department of Homeland Security)
topics: F-1 students, M-1 students, SEVIS, DSO, student visa, travel, change of status, student benefits
text:
Overview

All F and M students studying in the United States must have a Form I-20, "Certificate of Eligibility for Nonimmigrant Student Status."

Once accepted into a Student and Exchange Visitor Program (SEVP)-certified school, students receive a Form I-20 from their Designated School Official (DSO).

Students must also obtain a separate Form I-20 for each eligible dependent who will accompany them to the United States.

The type of Form I-20 issued determines:
- The type of student visa (F-1 or M-1) that may be obtained.
- The immigration status the student must maintain while in the United States.

The student and the DSO must sign the Form I-20. Students under 18 years of age must also have a parent or legal guardian sign the form.

Importance of Form I-20

The Form I-20 is an important immigration document that should be kept safe throughout a student's academic program. It is required for multiple immigration and educational processes.

Paying the I-901 SEVIS Fee

Students must

title: Students and the Form I-20
source: Study in the States (U.S. Department of Homeland Security)
topics: F-1 students, M-1 students, SEVIS, DSO, student visa, travel, change of status, student benefits
text:
hroughout a student's academic program. It is required for multiple immigration and educational processes.

Paying the I-901 SEVIS Fee

Students must first receive a Form I-20 before paying the mandatory I-901 Student and Exchange Visitor Information System (SEVIS) Fee.

Information required to pay the fee includes:
- Full name.
- Address.
- Date of birth.
- Email address.
- Country of birth.
- Country of citizenship.
- School Code listed on the Form I-20.
- SEVIS Identification Number listed on the Form I-20.

The I-901 SEVIS Fee must be paid before entering the United States.

Applying for a Student Visa

The Form I-20 lists the program start date.

Important requirements include:
- Students may enter the United States no earlier than 30 days before the program start date.
- F-1 and M-1 visas may be issued up to 365 days before the program begins.
- The visa category must match the Form I-20 (F-1 or M-1).
- Students are generally expected to present the original Form I-20 during the

title: Students and the Form I-20
source: Study in the States (U.S. Department of Homeland Security)
topics: F-1 students, M-1 students, SEVIS, DSO, student visa, travel, change of status, student benefits
text:
ram begins.
- The visa category must match the Form I-20 (F-1 or M-1).
- Students are generally expected to present the original Form I-20 during the visa interview.
- In limited circumstances, a consular officer may accept a copy before the original is received.

Entering the United States

Students should carry the signed original Form I-20 with them when traveling.

Do not pack the Form I-20 in checked luggage.

At the port of entry, a U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) officer may request the Form I-20.

Students may enter the United States up to 30 days before the program start date shown on the Form I-20.

Changing Status in the United States

Individuals already in the United States under another nonimmigrant classification must obtain a Form I-20 before applying to U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) to change status to F or M.

Applying for Student Benefits

The Form I-20 serves as proof of lawful enrollment in a U.S. educational program.

It may be require

title: Students and the Form I-20
source: Study in the States (U.S. Department of Homeland Security)
topics: F-1 students, M-1 students, SEVIS, DSO, student visa, travel, change of status, student benefits
text:
status to F or M.

Applying for Student Benefits

The Form I-20 serves as proof of lawful enrollment in a U.S. educational program.

It may be required when applying for benefits available to F and M students, including:
- Driver's licenses.
- Social Security numbers.
- Other student-related benefits.

When to Obtain a New Form I-20
t
A Designated School Official (DSO) may issue a new Form I-20 when:
- The original form is lost, destroyed, or misplaced.
- A travel endorsement is needed.
- The student's SEVIS status changes (for example, from Initial to Active).
- There is a significant change to student information, including:
  - Personal information.
  - Program of study.
  - Optional Practical Training (OPT).
  - Other material updates to the student's record.

Redesigned Form I-20

Beginning July 1, 2016, the redesigned Form I-20 became mandatory for:
- Student visa applications.
- Entry into the United States.
- International travel.
- Applications for nonimmigrant student benefit

title: Students and the Form I-20
source: Study in the States (U.S. Department of Homeland Security)
topics: F-1 students, M-1 students, SEVIS, DSO, student visa, travel, change of status, student benefits
text:
me mandatory for:
- Student visa applications.
- Entry into the United States.
- International travel.
- Applications for nonimmigrant student benefits.

The previous barcode version of Form I-20 is no longer valid.

Designated School Officials (DSOs) were responsible for providing updated paper copies with original signatures to all F-1 and M-1 students.

title: Maintaining Status
source: Study in the States (U.S. Department of Homeland Security)
document_types: F-1 Student Status, M-1 Student Status
topics: maintaining status, F-1 students, M-1 students, DSO, SEVIS, employment, CPT, OPT, travel, program completion
text:
Overview

While studying in the United States, F-1 and M-1 students must maintain their nonimmigrant student status. Maintaining status means fulfilling the purpose for which the student visa was issued and following all regulations associated with that status.

F-1 students attend academic programs, while M-1 students attend vocational programs. Because these programs differ, eligibility for benefits and the length of authorized stay also differ.

Arrival Requirements

Upon arriving in the United States, F-1 and M-1 students must:
- Enter the United States no more than 30 days before the program start date.
- Contact their Designated School Official (DSO) immediately after entering the United States.
- Check in with their DSO no later than the program start date listed on Form I-20.

Academic Requirements

While enrolled, students must:
- Attend all classes.
- Maintain normal academic progress.
- Maintain a full course of study each academic term.
- Contact their DSO immediately if th

title: Maintaining Status
source: Study in the States (U.S. Department of Homeland Security)
document_types: F-1 Student Status, M-1 Student Status
topics: maintaining status, F-1 students, M-1 students, DSO, SEVIS, employment, CPT, OPT, travel, program completion
text:
- Attend all classes.
- Maintain normal academic progress.
- Maintain a full course of study each academic term.
- Contact their DSO immediately if they cannot maintain full-time enrollment.
- Speak with their DSO before dropping a class.
- Request a program extension before the program end date listed on Form I-20 if additional time is needed to complete the program.

Annual Vacation for F-1 Students

F-1 students may qualify for annual vacation after completing at least one full academic year at an SEVP-certified school.

Students must:
- Intend to enroll in the following academic term.
- Follow their school's annual vacation policy.

During an authorized annual vacation, students may enroll in as many, as few, or no courses.

Employment and Practical Training for F-1 Students

F-1 students may work only with proper authorization.

Unauthorized employment is a violation of student status and may result in:
- Immediate departure from the United States.
- Future immigration consequence

title: Maintaining Status
source: Study in the States (U.S. Department of Homeland Security)
document_types: F-1 Student Status, M-1 Student Status
topics: maintaining status, F-1 students, M-1 students, DSO, SEVIS, employment, CPT, OPT, travel, program completion
text:
thorized employment is a violation of student status and may result in:
- Immediate departure from the United States.
- Future immigration consequences, including possible inability to re-enter.

Curricular Practical Training (CPT)
- Available during the student's program of study.
- Must be an integral part of the established curriculum.
- Employment must directly relate to the student's major field of study.
- Authorization is provided through the student's DSO according to school policy.

Optional Practical Training (OPT)
- Temporary employment directly related to the student's field of study.
- May occur during or after completion of the academic program.
- Requires proper authorization.

Students should consult their DSO before accepting any employment.

Practical Training for M-1 Students

M-1 students are generally not authorized to work during their program of study.

After completing their vocational program, M-1 students may apply for practical training.

Practical training r

title: Maintaining Status
source: Study in the States (U.S. Department of Homeland Security)
document_types: F-1 Student Status, M-1 Student Status
topics: maintaining status, F-1 students, M-1 students, DSO, SEVIS, employment, CPT, OPT, travel, program completion
text:
o work during their program of study.

After completing their vocational program, M-1 students may apply for practical training.

Practical training requires:
- A recommendation from the DSO.
- Authorization from U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS).

Unauthorized employment may result in:
- Immediate departure from the United States.
- Possible future restrictions on re-entering the United States.

Maintaining Status After Program Completion

F-1 Students

After completing the academic program and any authorized practical training:
- Students have a 60-day grace period to depart the United States or take other authorized action.

Possible options include:
- Transfer to another SEVP-certified school.
- Begin a higher educational program.
- Apply for a change of nonimmigrant status (such as H-1B, O, or P status).

M-1 Students

After completing the vocational program:
- Students have a 30-day grace period to depart the United States.
- Students may remain only until the "Ad

title: Maintaining Status
source: Study in the States (U.S. Department of Homeland Security)
document_types: F-1 Student Status, M-1 Student Status
topics: maintaining status, F-1 students, M-1 students, DSO, SEVIS, employment, CPT, OPT, travel, program completion
text:
After completing the vocational program:
- Students have a 30-day grace period to depart the United States.
- Students may remain only until the "Admit Until" date shown on Form I-94 unless an extension has been approved by USCIS.

Working with Your Designated School Official (DSO)

The DSO is the primary resource for questions regarding immigration status and maintaining student status.

F-1 students should consult their DSO before:
- Changing their major, program, or degree level.
- Changing educational level.
- Transferring schools.
- Taking a leave of absence.
- Taking a break from school.
- Traveling outside the United States.
- Moving to a new address.
- Requesting a program extension.

M-1 students should consult their DSO before:
- Taking a break from school.
- Traveling outside the United States.
- Moving to a new address.
- Requesting a program extension.

Contacting SEVP

If a DSO cannot resolve a student's question, F-1 and M-1 students may contact the Student and Exchang

title: Maintaining Status
source: Study in the States (U.S. Department of Homeland Security)
document_types: F-1 Student Status, M-1 Student Status
topics: maintaining status, F-1 students, M-1 students, DSO, SEVIS, employment, CPT, OPT, travel, program completion
text:
questing a program extension.

Contacting SEVP

If a DSO cannot resolve a student's question, F-1 and M-1 students may contact the Student and Exchange Visitor Program (SEVP) directly by email at:

sevp@ice.dhs.gov

Students should include:
- A description of their situation.
- Any questions regarding their status or immigration requirements.

SEVP responds to inquiries in the order they are received. Response times vary depending on inquiry volume.

title: Form I-797: Types and Functions
source: U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS)
topics: notices of action, receipt notices, approvals, requests for evidence, biometrics, interviews, transportation letter, immigration benefits
text:
USCIS uses several versions of Form I-797 to communicate with applicants and petitioners or to convey an immigration benefit. Form I-797 is not a form that applicants complete or submit.

Form I-797, Notice of Action
- Issued to communicate the receipt or approval of an application or petition.

Form I-797A, Notice of Action
- Issued to an applicant as a replacement Form I-94.

Form I-797B, Notice of Action
- Issued for the approval of an alien worker petition.

Form I-797C, Notice of Action
- Issued to communicate:
  - Receipt or rejection of applications or petitions.
  - Transfer of files.
  - Fingerprint (biometric) appointments.
  - Interview appointments and rescheduled appointments.
  - Additional actions.
  - Reopening of cases.

Form I-797D
- Accompanies benefit cards.

Form I-797E, Notice of Action
- Issued to request additional evidence.

Form I-797F, Transportation Letter
- Issued overseas to allow applicants to travel.
